entries.intro: I've been looking forward to the harvest feast all season. It's the one time everyone actually relaxes.
details: Last year I ate so much I had to lie down in the grass for an hour. No regrets.
The kids get so excited they can barely sleep the night before. Mine was up at dawn asking if it was time yet.
reactions: You need days like that. Otherwise what's the point of all the work?

entries.intro: The food at the festival is always the best part. Everyone brings something and you eat until you can't move.
details: There's a woman who makes these honey cakes that I think about for weeks afterward. Soft, warm, perfect.
The stew pot is always going. You just walk up, grab a bowl, and nobody asks whose it is.
reactions: Food always tastes better when you're eating it with everyone else.

statCheck.pass: The trick is pacing yourself. Start with the bread and cheese, save the sweets for later, and don't fill up on stew too early.
statCheck.fail: I never have a strategy. I just eat whatever's in front of me until I regret it.

entries.intro: Someone always drinks too much at the celebration. Last time it was the carpenter. Fell asleep on a hay bale and nobody noticed until morning.
details: He said the ale was stronger than he expected. It's the same ale every year.
His wife wasn't pleased. I could hear her all the way from my house.
reactions: It's the same story every time. You'd think people would learn, but celebrations have a short memory.

entries.intro: The music at the gathering was actually pretty good this year. The fiddler knew what he was doing.
details: He played a slow tune near the end of the night that had people swaying whether they meant to or not.
A few of the older folks got up and danced. I hadn't seen some of them move like that in years.
reactions: Good music makes everything feel right, even if it's just for an evening.

entries.intro: I skipped the festival this year. Just wasn't in the mood for all the noise and commotion.
details: I could hear it from my house. The laughing, the music, the shouting. Sounded like a good time, honestly.
My neighbor brought me a plate of food afterward. Didn't ask why I wasn't there. Just left it at the door.
reactions: Maybe next time. Some years you just want the quiet.

entries.intro: The bonfire got a bit out of hand last time. Someone stacked the wood too high and sparks were flying everywhere.
details: A few folks had to stamp out embers on the grass. Nothing caught, but it was close.
The person who built it just stood there grinning. Proud of himself, the fool.
reactions: It's all fun until someone's roof catches a spark. But nobody thinks about that after a few drinks.

statCheck.pass: They built it too close to the trees this time. A good ten paces farther and it wouldn't have been a problem.
statCheck.fail: I don't know much about building fires. It looked big. That's about all I can say.

entries.intro: The competitions are my favorite part of any celebration. Nothing serious. Just people showing off and having a laugh.
details: There's always an arm-wrestling contest. The smith wins every year and acts surprised about it every year.
Someone organized a foot race last time. Half the runners tripped over each other at the start.
reactions: It's good to see people being silly. You don't get enough of that on a normal day.

statCheck.pass: The trick to arm wrestling the smith is to catch him after he's eaten. Slower reflexes, heavier arm. Still probably won't win, though.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how anyone beats the smith. The man's built like a wall.

entries.intro: The cleanup after a celebration is always miserable. Everyone disappears and it's the same five people picking up the mess.
details: Last time I found a single boot in the bushes. Nobody claimed it.
The square looked like a storm hit it. Broken cups, spilled food, someone's scarf hanging from a post.
reactions: I love the celebration, but I'm starting to resent the morning after.
